cataloging_subject.label: The Repressor of Over Much Blaming of the Clergy
cataloging_subject.scope_note: Reginald Pecock's single five-part Middle English prose work, including its prologue, as printed on pp. 1–565 of Churchill Babington's two-volume 1860 edition and encoded in U-M/CME nodes AHB1325.0001.001:3–:8. The subject excludes the edition front matter in nodes :1–:2 and the back matter in node :9: excerpts from John Bury's Gladius Salomonis, the Abbreviatio Reginaldi Pecock, an extract from Thomas Gascoigne's theological dictionary, the glossary, and the index. It also excludes Babington's editorial notes as independent work content, the two print volumes and their scans, the U-M OCR and page images, Cambridge University Library MS Kk.4.26 as a physical witness, and the checked-in repository XML as distinct transmission layers.

catalog_summary.title: The Repressor of Over Much Blaming of the Clergy
catalog_summary.author: Reginald Pecock
catalog_summary.date: Composed circa 1449; Kk.4.26, called the only extant manuscript by Binski and Zutshi, dated c. 1449–57; first printed in 1860
catalog_summary.region: England; probably begun at Whittington College in London, but the exact composition history remains qualified

identifiers.scheme: Library of Congress call number in U-M header

titles.value: The Repressor of Over Much Blaming of the Clergy
titles.supplied_by: Conventional modern title normalized from Babington's 1860 title page

titles.value: The repressing of ouer miche wijting the clergie
notes: Pecock gives this self-name near the end of the prologue.

titles.value: The represser of ouer myche blamyng the clergie
notes: This is the title form in the work's closing sentence.

titles.value: The repressor of over much blaming of the clergy

agents.name: Reginald Pecock
notes: The U-M/CME header's 'Recock, Reginald' is retained as a source-record error/variant; it is not the normalized author name.
Approximate life dates commonly given as c. 1395–c. 1460 are not used as the work date.

agents.name: Churchill Babington
notes: Editor of the two-volume 1860 first print from Cambridge University Library MS Kk.4.26.

scope.description: The complete Repressor text on printed pp. 1–565, transcribed from Kk.4.26 with the editorial interventions described in Babington's introduction

assertion.notes: The display value preserves the sources' approximate c. 1449 dating without converting it into an exact normalized interval.

notes: This work date is separate from Binski and Zutshi's c. 1449–57 date for production of the surviving manuscript.

date_statements.display: First printed in Churchill Babington's two-volume edition in 1860

assertion.notes: Kelly says the Repressor was begun while Pecock was still at Whittington College; that supports a qualified London beginning, not a complete composition itinerary.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: The U-M header's generic code eng is normalized to enm for Pecock's fifteenth-century text.
Latin hymn lines and four Middle English verse lines tagged L in the XML occur only in Babington's editorial NOTE1 apparatus and therefore do not create a second work language or a verse component.

form_statements.description: A five-part prose treatise with a prose prologue. All 48 XML L elements belong to Babington's editorial notes rather than Pecock's work text.

subject_statements.label: Reason, Scripture, interpretation, and church authority
Images, pilgrimage, relics, liturgy, and accusations of idolatry
Clerical property, landed endowment, revenues, and confiscation
Papacy, episcopacy, church hierarchy, and ecclesiastical law
Religious orders and other contested ecclesiastical practices

summaries.value: Pecock answers lay and Lollard criticism of eleven clerical practices by insisting that blame must be grounded in patient, demonstrable doctrine. The first part develops his general case for reasoned moral judgment and against treating private scriptural interpretation as self-validating. The remaining parts defend images and pilgrimage; relics and liturgical practices; clerical landholding and endowment; papal and episcopal hierarchy and church law; and religious orders, while referring several remaining controversies to his other writings. The work does not excuse genuine clerical wrongdoing: its polemical target is criticism Pecock regards as unproved or misdirected.

The cataloged work is the prologue and five parts, XML nodes AHB1325.0001.001:3–:8 and Babington pp. 1–565. The source XML places both 1860 volume title pages together before a merged page sequence. Volume I carries the work through Part III, chapter 4, p. 302; Volume II resumes with Part III, chapter 5, p. 303. Node :9 begins the excluded multi-work back matter.

extent.description: One intellectual prose work, counted once. Its 89 structural divisions are the prologue container, five part containers, and 83 nested chapter divisions: 20 + 20 + 19 + 9 + 15. Across nodes :3–:8 the XML contains 6 DIV1, 83 DIV2, 89 HEAD, 770 P, 1,043 NOTE1, 21 Q1, 566 PB, no LG, and 48 L. All 48 L are nested inside 16 Q1 in eight Babington NOTE1 apparatus entries: 44 Latin hymn or liturgical lines on printed pp. 200–205 and four Middle English verse lines on p. 323. None is intrinsic verse in Pecock's prose work. The intellectual text occupies printed pp. 1–565; U-M sequence 110 is a preceding blank structurally placed in the prologue node, while sequences 111–675 carry those 565 printed text pages.

content_parts.label: The Repressor of Over Much Blaming of the Clergy
content_parts.summary: The single cataloged intellectual work: Pecock's Middle English prose Repressor, comprising a prologue and five structurally nested parts with 83 chapters.

locators.value: AHB1325.0001.001:3–:8; sequence 110 is a preceding blank inside :3 and target text is at sequences 111–675
begins fol. 1 and ends on the final numbered leaf among fols. 1–190; exact terminal side unresolved

notes: The root is the only count-bearing content part; the prologue, five part containers, and 83 chapters describe internal structure rather than additional works.

content_parts.label: Prologue
content_parts.summary: Adapts 2 Timothy 4:2 as an instruction that blame requires patience and doctrine, condemns ungrounded attacks on the clergy, identifies eleven disputed practices, names the book, and announces its five-part plan.

locators.value: AHB1325.0001.001:3; U-M sequences 110–114, with 110 a preceding blank and text on 111–114

content_parts.label: Part I
content_parts.summary: Twenty chapters establish the general argumentative framework: moral law and reason are not reducible to Scripture, scriptural meaning requires learned interpretation, and neither humble reading nor zeal makes a critic infallible. Pecock uses this framework to answer broad accusations against clerical governance.

content_parts.label: Part II
content_parts.summary: Twenty chapters turn to disputed devotional practices, principally the use of images, pilgrimage, relics, and accusations of idolatry, while also treating hymns, ceremonies, and related liturgical evidence.
notes: The 44 L elements in this part are Latin lines inside Babington's NOTE1 apparatus, not verse authored by Pecock.

content_parts.label: Part III
content_parts.summary: Nineteen chapters defend clerical possession of landed property and revenues through scriptural, patristic, and practical arguments, and address simony, avarice, the Donation of Constantine, ecclesiastical courts, and proposals for confiscation.

locators.value: AHB1325.0001.001:6; U-M sequences 385–525; chapter 4 ends at sequence 412 / p. 302 and chapter 5 begins at sequence 413 / p. 303

notes: The four L elements on printed p. 323 are a Middle English verse quotation inside Babington's NOTE1 apparatus.

content_parts.label: Part IV
content_parts.summary: Nine chapters defend differentiated ecclesiastical hierarchy, including papal and episcopal authority, and the church's authority to make laws and canons.

content_parts.label: Part V
content_parts.summary: Fifteen chapters defend the existence and governance of religious orders, then refer objections concerning several remaining practices—saintly and priestly intercession, costly church ornament, sacraments, oaths, warfare, and capital punishment—to Pecock's other books before ending with the work's explicit.

evidence.citation: Pinned CME XML for U-M item AHB1325.0001.001
evidence.locator: FILEDESC, ENCODINGDESC, and TEXT/BODY/DIV1 nodes :1–:9; XPath counts and manual text inspection on 2026-07-16
evidence.summary: Supports the exact repository identifier, U-M header title and author error, 2006 uncorrected-OCR statement, title self-identifications, five-part structure, 83 chapter count, node/page-image boundaries, editorial-note verse audit, and exclusion of front and back matter.

evidence.citation: Reginald Pecock, The Repressor of Over Much Blaming of the Clergy, ed. Churchill Babington, vol. I, Rerum Britannicarum Medii Aevi Scriptores 19 (London: Longman, Green, Longman, and Roberts, 1860)
evidence.locator: title page; introduction pp. lxii–lxvi; Summary of Contents pp. lxxxvii–xcviii; text pp. 1–302; PDF pp. 113–414 for the target
evidence.summary: Supports title, editor, first publication, direct transcription from Kk.4.26, editorial practices, manuscript description and provenance discussion, and the prologue through Part III chapter 4.

evidence.citation: Reginald Pecock, The Repressor of Over Much Blaming of the Clergy, ed. Churchill Babington, vol. II, Rerum Britannicarum Medii Aevi Scriptores 19 (London: Longman, Green, Longman, and Roberts, 1860)
evidence.locator: title page; text pp. 303–565; PDF pp. 13–275 for the target; excluded back matter begins after PDF p. 276
evidence.summary: Supports the exact volume transition, Part III chapter 5 through the Part V explicit, and separation of the target from the distinct works and apparatus that follow.

evidence.citation: A Catalogue of the Manuscripts Preserved in the Library of the University of Cambridge, vol. III (Cambridge: University Press, 1858), no. 2041, pp. 673–674
evidence.summary: Describes Kk. IV. 26 as 190 vellum leaves excluding one blank at each end, double-columned with 37 lines, beginning on fol. 1 and ending on the final numbered leaf; attributes the work to Pecock and records later hands and names.

evidence.citation: Paul Binski and Patrick Zutshi, with Stella Panayotova, Western Illuminated Manuscripts: A Catalogue of the Collection in Cambridge University Library (Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2011)
evidence.locator: Introduction and list of manuscripts, no. 256
evidence.summary: Calls Kk.4.26 the only extant Repressor manuscript, says it was probably corrected by the author, and dates manuscript no. 256 c. 1449–57. The catalog's method treats such dates as manuscript-production dates, not automatically as work-composition dates.

evidence.citation: Jennifer Anh-Thư Tran Smith, Reginald Pecock and Vernacular Theology in Pre-Reformation England (PhD diss., University of California, Los Angeles, 2012)
evidence.locator: discussion of work chronology and Appendix D, pp. 299–301
evidence.summary: Dates the Repressor c. 1449 and reports direct inspection of two correction hands, minor rather than substantive correction, and Thomas Atkyns's later annotation, while questioning a trial-copy interpretation.

evidence.citation: Henry Ansgar Kelly, ‘Judicial Processes for and against Bishop Reginald Pecock: New Perspectives on the Mechanisms of His Downfall,’ Viator 52.1 (2021): 359–430, doi:10.1484/J.VIATOR.5.130891
evidence.summary: Dates the Repressor ca. 1449, identifies the surviving Kk.4.26 copy, says the work was begun while Pecock was still at Whittington College, and discusses the 1457 exhibition note cautiously.

evidence.citation: Middle English Dictionary bibliography record BIB2730, ‘(c1449) Pecock Repr. (Cmb Kk.4.26)’
evidence.summary: Provides the current MED bibliography label c. 1449 and identifies the witness as Cambridge Kk.4.26.

open_questions.question: What is the exact chronology and location of composition beyond the conventional c. 1449 date and Kelly's statement that the work was begun while Pecock was at Whittington College?
next_steps: Review the textual chronology and documentary arguments in current Pecock scholarship before converting the qualified London beginning into a full place-of-composition claim.
Keep the c. 1449 work date separate from the c. 1449–57 production date assigned to Kk.4.26.

notes: The work boundary follows Pecock's prologue and explicit, not Babington's whole two-volume publication or U-M's whole 822-image sequence.
The source header's 'Recock, Reginald' is preserved as an error/variant while the responsible author is normalized as Reginald Pecock.

The Repressor is one intellectual prose work and is counted once. Its 89 structural divisions are one prologue container, five part containers, and 83 nested chapter divisions: Part I 20, Part II 20, Part III 19, Part IV 9, and Part V 15.
Raw L counts do not determine form: all 48 L occur in Babington's notes and intrinsic work verse_lines remains zero.
The only-extant-manuscript claim is attributed to Binski and Zutshi's Cambridge catalog; it is not broadened into a claim that no fragmentary or lost evidence can ever emerge.
